event: Austronesian-speaking voyagers settle the Gilbert Islands, establishing the earliest Micronesian communities on the atolls.
Permanent Micronesian settlement is firmly established across the Gilbert Islands, with distinct maneaba (meeting house) social structures developing.
Samoan and Tongan voyagers arrive in the southern Gilberts, intermixing with existing populations and influencing local culture and chiefly systems.
Spanish explorer Pedro Fernandes de Queirós sights Butaritari and Makin, among the first recorded European encounters with the islands.
British Commodore John Byron sights Nikunau during his circumnavigation aboard HMS Dolphin.
British captains Thomas Gilbert and John Marshall pass through the archipelago, after whom the Gilbert Islands are later named.
Russian explorer Adam von Krusenstern names the island chain the Gilbert Islands in honour of Captain Thomas Gilbert.
Whalers and traders begin frequenting the islands, introducing trade goods, alcohol, firearms, and new diseases.
American Protestant missionaries of the ABCFM, led by Hiram Bingham II, begin Christianising the northern Gilberts.
Catholic and Protestant missions expand across the islands, and blackbirding labour recruiters take Islanders to plantations abroad.
Catholic Sacred Heart missionaries establish a permanent presence, intensifying religious conversion across the southern islands.
Britain declares a protectorate over the Gilbert and Ellice Islands, formalising colonial control under Captain Edward Davis.
Phosphate is discovered on Banaba (Ocean Island), leading to the founding of the Pacific Phosphate Company and decades of mining.
The Gilbert and Ellice Islands are formally annexed as a British Crown Colony, with Banaba added soon after.
The Phoenix Islands are incorporated into the colony, and the Phoenix Islands Settlement Scheme begins relocating Gilbertese settlers.
Japanese forces seize Makin and Tarawa shortly after entering the Pacific War, occupying the northern Gilberts.
Japanese troops execute captured coastwatchers and missionaries on Tarawa as they fortify the atoll.
The Battle of Tarawa sees fierce US Marine fighting at Betio against entrenched Japanese defenders in one of the war's bloodiest assaults.
Britain conducts Operation Grapple hydrogen-bomb tests near Kiritimati (Christmas Island), exposing personnel and islanders to fallout.
The United States carries out Operation Dominic nuclear tests over Kiritimati during the height of the Cold War.
An advisory council and the first steps toward representative government are introduced in the colony.
The colony gains a measure of internal self-government with an elected legislative assembly and a chief minister.
The Ellice Islands separate from the colony to become Tuvalu, reflecting ethnic differences between Polynesians and Micronesians.
The Gilbert Islands attain full internal self-government as a precursor to independence.
The Banaban people pursue legal claims in British courts over phosphate-mining damage to Ocean Island.
Kiribati becomes an independent republic on 12 July with Ieremia Tabai as its first president and beretitenti.
The United States relinquishes claims to the Phoenix and Line Islands under the Treaty of Tarawa.
Phosphate mining on Banaba ceases as deposits are exhausted, ending a major source of colonial-era revenue.
President Ieremia Tabai is re-elected, consolidating Kiribati's early democratic governance.
Teburoro Tito is elected president, beginning a period of continuity in the young republic's leadership.
Kiribati moves the International Date Line eastward so all its islands share the same calendar day, making it first to greet each new day.
Kiribati is admitted as a full member of the United Nations on 14 September.
Kiritimati's eastern Line Islands are promoted as the first land to enter the new millennium under the realigned date line.
Anote Tong wins the presidency and begins advocating internationally on climate change and rising sea levels.
Kiribati establishes the Phoenix Islands Protected Area, one of the world's largest marine reserves.
The Phoenix Islands Protected Area is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its pristine coral ecosystems.
President Anote Tong's government purchases land in Fiji at Natoavatu Estate as insurance against climate-driven displacement.
Kiribati citizen Ioane Teitiota seeks recognition as a climate-change refugee in New Zealand, a landmark legal case.
Taneti Maamau is elected president, later pursuing closer ties with major Pacific powers.
Kiribati switches diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to the People's Republic of China.
President Taneti Maamau is re-elected after a campaign shaped by debates over development and ties with China.
Kiribati announces its withdrawal from the Pacific Islands Forum amid a regional leadership dispute, later reversing course.
Kiribati continues to face severe climate threats including coastal erosion, saltwater intrusion, and freshwater scarcity across its low-lying atolls.
